references.title: Evaluating sources of course information and models of representation on a variety of institutional prediction tasks

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Grade at the end of a course in a Semester
extracted.Students:
All students between Fall 2008 and Fall 2015 → training data

Data from Spring 2016 → Test data
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Different forms of preprocessing are attempted based on different sources:

LSTM, BOW and Course2Vec.
extracted.Comments:
Holdout Method implementation follows a temporal logic to split the data. 

Both courses  and students are used to create embeddings.

The sequential information of the semesters relevealed itself to be extremely valuable as it led to the best performance.

Work is, by no means, exclusively using data for this purpose, but none of the other tasks fall into the objective of this work.
extracted.Limitations:
Data from a single institution

No evaluation of more classical models

Evaluation metric for task of predicting grade is not ideal (especially in multiclass contexts).

No mention on how the class imbalance is addressed (if it is at all).

annotations.text: More availability of data has contributed to a proliferation of research on predicting next course. The paper provides a comprehensive revieww of data sources and models for different prediction tasks.
Authors evaluate different sources and models to predict:

Results show that enrollment-based graph embeddings perform better at predicting course pre-requisites. In addition, descriptions, syllabi and course2vec combined achieve the best predictive capabilities for course similarity.
